---
title: "Exercise: Hello, DNA"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Before we do any bioinformatics, make sure `cargo test` works on your machine. Your task is to write a one-line function that returns a string — nothing more. If this exercise compiles and tests pass, your Rust toolchain is healthy and you are ready for the real exercises.

## Background

Every Rust function declares its return type after the `->` in its signature; the body must produce a value of exactly that type. This function returns a `&'static str` [a fixed string baked into the binary at compile time, with a reference that is valid for the entire run of the program].

## The exercise

Open the starter project:

```bash
cd day1/ex-hello-dna
```

You will see a single Rust source file at `src/main.rs`. It contains a function `first_message() -> &'static str` with a `// TODO` body, a `main` that prints whatever the function returns, and one unit test.

Fill in the TODO so that `cargo test` passes. The function must return exactly the string `"Hello, ACGT!"`.

## How to run

From inside `day1/ex-hello-dna/`:

```bash
cargo test            # run the test — it should FAIL the first time
# now edit src/main.rs and replace the TODO
cargo test            # run the test again — it should PASS
cargo run             # print the greeting
```

When the test passes, `cargo test` will print:

```
test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

and your program should print:

```
$ cargo run
Hello, ACGT!
```

## Hint

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint — What goes in the function body?"}
The body of the function is just the string literal itself:

```rust
"Hello, ACGT!"
```

No [`return`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/keyword.return.html) keyword is needed — in Rust, the last expression in a block is the value of the block, and so the value of the function. (See the Rust Book on [expressions vs. statements](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch03-03-how-functions-work.html#statements-and-expressions) if you want the formal rule.)
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
The full reference implementation lives in `day1/ex-hello-dna-solution/src/main.rs`. Try the one-line fix yourself first.
:::

## Where this leads

The next warm-up exercises take the same `cargo test` workflow and apply it to byte slices ([`&[u8]`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html)) and owned byte vectors ([`Vec<u8>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html)) before the proper exercises begin.
